data.title: Elastic outlines 12% revenue growth target for FY 2026 amid strong GenAI momentum and strategic partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elastic N.V. (ESTC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ashutosh Kulkarni highlighted a strong quarter for Elastic, stating, "We exceeded guidance across all revenue and profitability metrics, demonstrating our platform strength and the extraordinary efforts of our team." He noted revenue growth of 16%, Elastic cloud revenue growth of 23%, and a non-GAAP operating margin of 15%. Kulkarni emphasized the impact of sales strategy shifts, strong enterprise customer additions, and the growing influence of GenAI and consolidation as drivers for customer adoption.
  • Kulkarni described major customer wins, including an eight-figure expansion deal with a global financial institution and a multi-year seven-figure expansion with an international banking group, both leveraging Elastic solutions for observability and GenAI applications. He announced more than 2,000 Elastic Cloud customers using Elastic for GenAI use cases, with over 310 spending $100,000 or more annually.
  • Kulkarni also pointed to the general availability of the BBQ technology for vector compression and a new strategic collaboration with AWS, stating, "We announced a new five-year strategic collaboration agreement with AWS, which deepens our partnership through solution integrations, joint go-to-market, and marketing initiatives."
  • CFO Navam Welihinda remarked, "We finished the year strong and outperformed against the high end of both our revenue and profitability guidance. Our total revenue in the fourth quarter was $388 million, growing 16% on an as reported and constant currency basis." Welihinda noted subscription revenue of $362 million and Elastic Cloud revenue growth of 23%. He added, "Our current remaining performance obligations or CRPO in Q4...was approximately $1 billion and grew 18% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Welihinda provided guidance for fiscal year 2026, stating, "We expect total revenue in the range of $1.655 billion to $1.67 billion. Representing 12% year-over-year growth at the midpoint or 11% year-over-year constant currency growth at the midpoint." For Q1 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected to be between $396 million and $398 million, with non-GAAP diluted earnings per share in the range of $0.41 to $0.43. Non-GAAP operating margin is projected to be approximately 16% for the full year.
  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty, particularly extrapolating U.S. public sector pressures to the wider business as a conservative factor in guidance. Welihinda explained, "The lower end of our guidance range also assumes we see headwinds to consumption from Q2 to Q4, though we did not see them in Q4 of FY 2025."

Financial Results

  • Elastic reported total revenue of $388 million for Q4, with subscription revenue at $362 million and Elastic Cloud revenue growth of 23%. Gross margin for the quarter was 77% of revenue and operating margin was 15%.
  • Adjusted free cash flow margin for fiscal year 2025 ended at 19%. Welihinda stated, "We grew our adjusted free cash flow margin by approximately 600 basis points in fiscal year 2025."
  • The company ended FY 2025 with more than 1,510 customers spending over $100,000 and over 210 customers with more than $1 million in committed annual contract value.

Q&A

  • Pinjalim Bora, JP Morgan, asked about guidance conservatism. Welihinda responded, "I'm new to the business and I need to be prudent about the first guide...I've balanced the positive demand signals...with broadly macro uncertainty."
  • Bora also inquired about strategic partnerships. Kulkarni highlighted, "We are seeing real change in the market where we are being accepted as one of the most appropriate and favored vector databases out there."
  • Sanjit Singh, Morgan Stanley, questioned the state of RAG and cloud growth. Kulkarni stated, "RAG is being acknowledged as the solution that always makes sense...we feel very confident."
  • Singh also addressed sequential cloud revenue, with Welihinda attributing slower growth to the quarter having three fewer days and emerging seasonal patterns.
  • Tyler Radke, Citi, probed AI commitments and use cases. Kulkarni clarified, "25% of our million dollar customers are using us for some kind of AI workload." He noted emerging agentic AI use cases in sales, marketing, and legal sectors.
  • Howard Ma, Guggenheim Securities, sought clarity on cloud growth offsets. Kulkarni emphasized, "AI is helping us both in cloud and in self-managed," while Welihinda reinforced that both segments are expected to drive growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts consistently pressed for clarity on guidance conservatism, cloud growth drivers, and the impact of macro headwinds, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone regarding future visibility and growth sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident stance in prepared remarks and responded with caution and transparency during Q&A, using phrases like "we feel really good about the business overall" and "we have multiple paths to get to and exceed the guidance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current analyst sentiment focused more on risk, while management shifted from the prior quarter's upbeat tone to a more measured outlook, particularly regarding macro uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY 2026 projects 12% revenue growth at the midpoint, compared to 16% guidance for FY 2025 in the prior quarter, reflecting increased conservatism.
  • Cloud revenue growth in Q4 was 23%, down from 26% in Q3, attributed to fewer days in the quarter and emerging seasonal patterns.
  • The company added more than 45 net new customers with over $1 million in annual contract value compared to strong but unspecified growth in the previous quarter.
  • Management's tone shifted from highly optimistic in Q3 to more cautious and detail-oriented, explicitly citing macroeconomic and public sector risks.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from sales execution and product innovation in Q3 to guidance methodology, macro headwinds, and sustainability in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified U.S. public sector budget and personnel constraints as a source of sales cycle elongation and extrapolated similar potential impacts to broader guidance.
  • Macroeconomic uncertainty is explicitly cited as the main driver for conservative revenue guidance.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the underlying assumptions in guidance and the sustainability of cloud growth, reflecting concerns about visibility and external pressures.

Final Takeaway

Elastic's leadership underscored sustained momentum in GenAI, strategic partnerships, and customer expansion, while adopting a more cautious outlook for FY 2026 due to macroeconomic uncertainties and public sector headwinds. The company continues to prioritize innovation, cloud adoption, and enterprise expansion, with a balanced approach to growth and profitability as it enters the new fiscal year.

data.title: Cooper Companies outlines 10%–11.5% EPS growth for 2025 amid tighter revenue guidance and accelerated Myopia portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cooper Companies, Inc. (COO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Al White, President and CEO, emphasized, “This was another solid quarter with consolidated organic revenue growth of 7%, led by double-digits growth in both our daily silicone hydrogel lenses at CooperVision and our office and surgical portfolio at Cooper Surgical.” He noted ongoing operational improvements and “OpEx leverage that drove double-digits non-GAAP earnings growth.”
  • The company reported consolidated revenues of $1.002 billion, with CooperVision contributing $670 million and CooperSurgical $333 million. White highlighted, “MyDay continued growing double-digits with particular strength in torics and multifocals, and our innovative Energy’s offering.” MyDay Energys was launched in Canada, and the upgraded Clarity One Day Sphere with WetLoc technology was released in Japan.
  • Myopia management portfolio revenue grew 19%, with MySight up 35%. White stated, “We just launched MyDay Energys with its innovative digital boost technology in Canada...and early feedback is extremely positive.” The company is focusing on reducing upfront fitting barriers for MySight, introducing promotions such as an initial one to three months free.
  • CooperSurgical’s revenue growth was driven by “success in our surgical medical devices, labor and delivery portfolio and PARAGARD.” Fertility revenues were $127 million, up 3%, but White cited “market softness,” especially in Asia Pac, due to declining cycles.
  • White announced, “We’re tightening and raising at the midpoint” of revenue guidance, but also reducing growth expectations for contact lenses and fertility due to lower market assumptions.
  • Brian Andrews, CFO, stated, “Consolidated gross margin was 68%, up from 67.3%, driven by continuing efficiency gains and mix.” He added, “One-line item worth highlighting this quarter is R&D, where expenses increased 21%,” reflecting higher investments in both segments.

Outlook

  • The company updated fiscal 2025 revenue guidance to $4.11 billion to $4.15 billion, “up roughly 5.5% to 6.5%, or up 5% to 6% organically.”
  • CooperVision’s revenue guidance is $2.76 billion to $2.79 billion, with organic growth of 6% to 7%. CooperSurgical’s guidance is $1.35 billion to $1.36 billion, up 3.5% to 4.5% organically.
  • Non-GAAP EPS guidance was raised to $4.05 to $4.11, “growth of roughly 10% to 11.5% year-over-year.”
  • Management expects “organic growth in Q4 to be stronger than Q3.” Lowered growth assumptions for both contact lenses (now 4% to 6% vs. prior 5% to 7%) and fertility were explicitly driven by recent market data.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues were $1.002 billion, with gross margin at 68% and non-GAAP EPS at $0.96.
  • Operating expense increased 6%, but declined as a percent of revenue to 43.1%.
  • R&D expenses rose 21% due to new product development.
  • Operating income was up 11%, with operating margin at 24.9%.
  • Free cash flow was $18 million; CapEx was $78 million.
  • Net debt increased to $2.47 billion; leverage ratio improved to 1.9 times. The company repurchased 537,000 shares for $40.6 million.

Q&A

  • Jeff Johnson, Baird: Asked about contact lens market data and inventory. Al White responded, “Our FIT data remains strong. As a matter of fact, it's accelerating right now...we're starting to see a pickup in MyDay activity.”
  • Issie Kirby, Redburn Atlantic: Inquired about market growth assumptions and pricing. White replied, “Pricing is still sound...volume is still good. The mix shift...is driving a lot of the growth.” On fertility softness in Asia, he attributed it partly to cultural timing and tighter consumer spending.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked about consumer behavior and quarterly trends. White explained, “...if somebody is not buying a year's supply, but they're buying a three-month or six-month supply...all that stuff has an impact.” Andrews clarified that Q3 is expected below the guidance range, with Q4 above.
  • Jon Block, Stifel: Queried inventory trends and fertility. White said inventory pressure will persist this year. On fertility, “I think we probably swung that a little bit too far...I would be really surprised if Q3 and Q4 don't show strength.”
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: Questioned guidance conservatism. White admitted, “A portion of it to be fair is conservatism. I think that we needed to reset our numbers.”
  • Young Li, Jefferies: Asked about private label growth. White stated, “Private label biz for us at CooperVision growing a little bit faster than our branded business. No real surprise.”
  • Chris Pasquale, Nephron: Inquired about pricing. White said, “I think that pricing right now in the market is solid. I think future pricing is probably going to be a little bit higher than what it was pre-COVID.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the reasons for guidance reductions and sought clarity on inventory and market trends, reflecting a slightly negative to cautious tone.
  • Management acknowledged conservatism in guidance, market uncertainties, and inventory pressures, but maintained optimism on execution and product momentum. During prepared remarks, the tone was confident, but shifted to more cautious and practical during Q&A, with frequent references to market softness and inventory adjustments.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both management and analysts were less bullish, with more focus on downside risks and a prudent stance on forecasts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance narrowed and growth expectations for contact lenses and fertility were reduced, whereas the previous quarter maintained higher targets.
  • Management’s tone shifted from optimistic to cautiously conservative, with explicit acknowledgment of inventory and macro challenges. Analysts in both quarters probed market share and growth sustainability, but Q2 saw increased focus on market headwinds and guidance prudence.
  • Myopia management and private label momentum accelerated, while channel inventory and consumer behavior emerged as larger themes.
  • Margin improvement and cost controls remained key, with additional R&D investments highlighted this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Softness in fertility, particularly Asia Pac, due to declining cycles and clinic spending.
  • Channel inventory reductions across the industry are expected to pressure revenues throughout the year.
  • Tariffs pose a $4 million headwind to costs in 2025, with potential 3% earnings impact in 2026 absent mitigation.
  • Currency headwinds remain, though mitigated from earlier projections.
  • Management is monitoring consumer behavior—shorter supply purchases and hesitancy on upfront costs for MySight.

Final Takeaway

Cooper Companies delivered another quarter of solid operational execution, highlighted by revenue growth in its core portfolios and raised earnings guidance. However, the company adopted a more cautious approach by tightening and lowering revenue expectations due to softening market growth in contact lenses and fertility, ongoing inventory pressures, and external headwinds such as tariffs and currency effects. Key product launches and aggressive moves in myopia management are expected to drive stronger growth in the fourth quarter, with management remaining confident in market share gains and the long-term outlook despite near-term conservatism.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Costco signals continued price investment and targets 27 new warehouses in 2025 while expanding digital initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ron Vachris highlighted the company’s expansion, noting, "Since our last earnings call, we've opened nine warehouses, including relocation in Melbourne, Australia, our 37th warehouse in Japan and seven net new U.S. locations. We plan to open another 10 warehouses during the fourth fiscal quarter." He stated Costco expects "to open 27 new warehouses, including three relocations for a total of 24 net new buildings" in fiscal 2025, bringing the total to 914 worldwide.
  • Vachris emphasized the company’s focus on maintaining competitive pricing and member value amid a challenging macroeconomic backdrop, citing actions such as "lowering prices on some key items such as eggs, butter and olive oil" and expanded gas station hours, which led to "two of our all-time highest gallon weeks in the U.S. during the last month."
  • Vachris reported Kirkland Signature sales growth outpacing overall sales and described efforts to localize sourcing: "We continue to move more Kirkland Signature product sourcing into the countries or regions where the items are sold, and this has helped bring us to lower cost and mitigate some of the potential impacts of tariffs."
  • The CEO referenced digital and technology investments, including the launch of a Buy Now Pay Later program with Affirm: "This new program allows our members greater access to the Costco values on big ticket items... at exclusive rates for the Costco members. While still early days, we've been pleased with the initial sales results."
  • CFO Gary Millerchip stated, "Net income for the third quarter came in at $1.9 billion or $4.28 per diluted share, up more than 13% from $1.68 billion or $3.78 per diluted share in the third quarter last year."

Outlook

  • Management expects to open 10 additional warehouses in Q4, including key international expansions in Sweden, Korea, and Canada.
  • Vachris stated, "As we look ahead to the remainder of the fiscal year, while the impacts of tariffs and the outlook for the economy in general remain unknown, we are confident in the ability of our operators and merchants to rise to the challenges and continue to offer great service and find consistent values for our members."
  • Millerchip indicated a potential future LIFO charge, saying, "Based on our LIFO accounting methodology, if the current rate of inflation is maintained until our fiscal year-end, we would have an additional $40 million to $50 million LIFO charge in the fourth quarter."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the third quarter were $61.96 billion, representing an 8% increase from $57.39 billion in the prior year’s third quarter.
  • U.S. comparable sales increased 6.6% (7.9% excluding gas deflation). E-commerce comparable sales rose 14.8% (15.7% adjusted for FX).
  • Membership fee income was $1.24 billion, up $117 million or 10.4% year-over-year. Renewal rates declined slightly to 92.7% in the U.S. and Canada and 90.2% worldwide, primarily due to "sign-ups from a Groupon promotion in the fall of 2023, entering the renewal rate calculation for the first time this quarter" (Millerchip).
  • Gross margin improved by 41 basis points to 11.25%. Millerchip cited "strong sales leverage benefited our payroll costs and spoilage results" and cost relief from some key commodity price declines.
  • SG&A rate rose to 9.16%, up 20 basis points year-over-year, reflecting "investment in employee wages, partially offset by sales leverage and productivity improvements." Capital expenditures in Q3 were approximately $1.13 billion.

Q&A

  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, asked about pricing strategy amid tariffs and competitive landscape. Vachris responded, "We take every advantage of every opportunity that we can lower prices and we've seen our competitive landscape improve slightly... we're going to continue to invest in price."
  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan, inquired about pull-forward in non-foods due to tariffs. Vachris explained, "Our buyers were very proactive, and they pulled a lot forward a lot of our summer goods... That allowed us to hold prices or come just slightly up on prices when we need it to be."
  • Michael Lasser, UBS, questioned margin sustainability and U.S. capacity. Millerchip noted, "We are cycling volume starting to flow through on a year-over-year basis, particularly online... we continue to see opportunity to grow in that space." Vachris added the focus on "improve the member experience in our high-volume warehouses" through new openings and technology pilots.
  • Multiple analysts probed LIFO accounting, digital engagement, and technology pilots. Millerchip detailed LIFO methodology and current inflation impacts, while Vachris described successful early results from "Scan & Go done by Costco" pilots.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on pricing posture, tariff impacts, and margin sustainability, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone, especially regarding the macro environment and operational capacity.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive stance, emphasizing agility, value delivery, and operational investment. Vachris consistently reiterated the company’s commitment to "continue to invest in price" and highlighted nimbleness in supply chain and sourcing strategies.
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone remains confident, but there is increased discussion of tariff volatility and digital expansion, while analysts’ questions show heightened focus on headwinds and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reiterated its focus on warehouse expansion and price leadership, but there is increased emphasis on mitigating tariff impacts through sourcing and inventory strategies versus Q2.
  • The number of planned new warehouses shifted slightly from 28 (Q2) to 27 (Q3), and membership renewal rates showed a slight decrease, attributed to digital and promotional sign-ups.
  • Gross margin expansion was more pronounced this quarter. SG&A leverage shifted from an improvement in Q2 to a slight increase in Q3, reflecting higher wage investments.
  • Analyst questions in Q3 were more focused on pricing, margin outlook, and the impact of tariffs, while digital and operational efficiency continued as recurring themes.
  • Management’s confidence in digital initiatives and technology pilots is more prominent this quarter, with explicit examples of early success and plans for broader rollout.

Risks and Concerns

  • Volatility and uncertainty around tariffs and macroeconomic conditions were cited as ongoing challenges.
  • Millerchip warned of a potential additional $40 million to $50 million LIFO charge if current inflation trends persist.
  • Declining renewal rates were attributed to digital promotions and new member cohorts, with management noting these new members “renew at a slightly lower rate.”
  • Management expressed confidence in mitigating risks via agile sourcing, price investments, and operational enhancements but acknowledged the dynamic and evolving environment.

Final Takeaway

Costco’s leadership underscored a strategy of aggressive price investment, continued global expansion, and technological innovation to drive member value and operational efficiency. The company’s agility in sourcing and inventory management is helping to offset tariff-related pressures, while digital engagement and technology pilots are poised to enhance the member experience. While renewal rates and inflation dynamics present operational challenges, Costco remains confident in its ability to deliver growth and value to its members and shareholders through disciplined execution and a focus on long-term opportunities.

data.title: Zscaler targets $3B ARR milestone and unveils Z-Flex to drive platform adoption amid strong Q3 performance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zscaler (ZS) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Jay Chaudhry, Chairman and CEO, highlighted "our strong Q3 results demonstrate growing demand for our platform and continued improvement in our sales productivity." He announced the company achieved over $1 billion in total contract value (TCV) bookings for the quarter and nearly $5 billion in remaining performance obligations (RPO). He also stated, "Our annual recurring revenue, or ARR, was approximately $2.9 billion, representing the third straight quarter of 23% year-over-year growth. We remain on track to reach $3 billion or more in ARR by the end of this quarter."
  • Chaudhry detailed the launch and early traction of the Z-Flex purchasing program, noting "Z-Flex commitments contributed over $65 million in TCV bookings" and explained that the program offers customers flexible scaling and procurement. He expects "the contribution from Z-Flex to grow meaningfully in the next fiscal year."
  • Product innovation was a focal point, with Chaudhry stating, "Our platform now secures over 50 million users," and emphasizing advances in Zero Trust Everywhere, Data Security Everywhere, and Agentic Operations, which are "approaching $1 billion" in combined ARR and growing faster than the overall business.
  • Remo Canessa, CFO, reported, "Revenue was $678 million, up 23% year-over-year and up 5% sequentially." He added, "Our annual recurring revenue, or ARR, exiting Q3 was approximately $2.9 billion. RPO grew 30% from a year ago to $4.978 billion."
  • Canessa also highlighted, "Total calculated billings grew 25% year-over-year to $785 million," and noted the company's free cash flow margin was 18% for the quarter.
  • Chaudhry announced the appointment of Kevin Rubin as the new Chief Financial Officer, with Canessa remaining as an advisor until fiscal year-end. Rubin commented, "I am incredibly excited to be joining Zscaler, the leader in cloud security. I believe with its expanding platform, Zscaler is well-positioned to benefit in an increasingly AI-driven enterprise security market."

Outlook

  • For Q4, Canessa guided revenue in the range of $705 million to $707 million, "reflecting a year-over-year growth of approximately 19%." Gross margin is expected to be approximately 80%, with operating profit between $152 million and $154 million, and earnings per share in the range of $0.79 to $0.80.
  • Full-year fiscal 2025 guidance was raised, with billings projected at $3.184 billion to $3.189 billion, revenue at $2.659 billion to $2.661 billion, operating profit between $573 million and $575 million, and EPS between $3.18 and $3.19. Free cash flow margin is expected at 25.5% to 26%.
  • Management stated, "With a large market opportunity and customers increasingly adopting the broader platform, we'll invest aggressively to position us for long-term growth and profitability."

Financial Results

  • Zscaler reported Q3 revenue of $678 million, up 23% year-over-year and 5% sequentially. RPO reached $4.978 billion. The company ended Q3 with approximately $3 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments.
  • Calculated billings were $785 million, a 25% increase year-over-year. Gross margin was 80.3%. Operating margin remained at 22% year-over-year.
  • The company ended the quarter with 642 customers over $1 million in ARR and 3,363 customers with over $100,000 in ARR. The 12-month trailing dollar-based net retention rate was 114%.
  • Canessa commented on the upcoming use of $675 million in cash for the Red Canary acquisition, expected to close in August 2025.

Q&A

  • Ittai Kidron, Oppenheimer & Company, asked about sales focus with the expanding portfolio and Z-Flex. Chaudhry explained that the sales organization employs a two-tier model, combining broad account coverage with specialist takeoff teams and said, "We're going to start with larger customers, then expand to the next level. There's nothing holding it back, but we like to be prudent, test things, learn it, and expand from there."
  • Mike Cikos, Needham, inquired about macro trends and deal scrutiny. Chaudhry responded, "Overall, the spending environment remains challenging. Macro is still tight, and we continue to see large deal scrutiny. Now, budgets are tight in general for IT, but for cyber, it's a little easier because cyber is a bigger priority."
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank, asked about Z-Flex's structure and future billing metrics. Canessa answered, "We're going to go to ARR in fiscal '26. So you're going to see a change from billing to ARR. I think that's really big for Zscaler."
  • Saket Kalia, Barclays, asked about the Red Canary acquisition's strategic fit. Chaudhry replied, "Red Canary brings a number of interesting things for us to move in that direction at a faster pace, so we can kind of accelerate our vision of becoming a leading player."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive tone, frequently congratulating management on results, new appointments, and strategic moves. Their questions focused on the scalability of new programs, macro impacts, and clarification of growth drivers, indicating active engagement and cautious optimism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using language such as "very excited," "great start," and expressing high expectations for new initiatives and acquisitions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained confident, with a continued focus on innovation and execution, while analysts remained inquisitive but supportive.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised from the previous quarter, with stronger full-year projections and higher billings and revenue targets.
  • The Z-Flex program was introduced as a new purchasing mechanism, contributing notably to bookings and signaling a shift in customer procurement flexibility.
  • Management announced a CFO transition, with Kevin Rubin joining and Remo Canessa moving to an advisory role.
  • Product momentum accelerated, especially in Zero Trust Everywhere, Data Security Everywhere, and Agentic Operations, with combined ARR approaching $1 billion and exceeding growth rates of the overall business.
  • Analysts continued to focus on macroeconomic headwinds, sales productivity, and product expansion, echoing previous quarter concerns but with more attention on new initiatives like Z-Flex and the Red Canary acquisition.
  • Management sentiment remained positive and future-focused, while analysts’ tone reflected cautious interest in operational execution and strategic investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing economic uncertainty, with Chaudhry stating, "Customers remain cautious about their IT spending due to ongoing economic uncertainty."
  • Canessa noted, "We continue to see large deal scrutiny," emphasizing that customer budgets remain tight and large deals undergo significant evaluation.
  • There is ongoing attention to the potential for variability in the dollar-based net retention rate as larger, bundled deals become more common.
  • The Red Canary acquisition was cited as neutral to FY '26 operating margin, with management expecting to retain approximately half of Red Canary's $140 million ARR post-integration.

Final Takeaway

Zscaler emphasized robust Q3 results, underscored by continued momentum in its Zero Trust platform and significant customer adoption via innovative programs such as Z-Flex. Management reaffirmed confidence in reaching or exceeding $3 billion in ARR in the next quarter and highlighted the strategic addition of Red Canary to accelerate security operations capabilities. The company’s focus on flexibility in procurement, expansion of AI-powered solutions, and a seamless CFO transition positions Zscaler for sustained growth, even as it navigates a cautious macro environment and evolving customer priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Eagle signals $275M capex plan and focuses on cleaner inventory amid Q1 loss and paused annual guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Jay Schottenstein, Executive Chairman and CEO, opened by stating, "we were disappointed with our first quarter performance. The margin impact, together with the $75 million inventory write-down, contributed to a $68 million adjusted operating loss for the quarter." He highlighted comparable sales declines at both American Eagle and Aerie, citing product misses and higher costs. Schottenstein shared, "We have been busy correcting and planning fall 2025 and spring 2026 seasons with all of our learnings top of mind." He also noted the closure of two fulfillment centers, which resulted in a $17 million non-GAAP charge but is expected to generate annualized savings of approximately $5 million.

  • Schottenstein emphasized, "annual guidance remains paused until we have greater visibility," but expressed confidence in future improvement. He announced the initiation of a $200 million accelerated share repurchase program and $31 million in open-market share repurchases, along with $22 million paid in cash dividends during the quarter.

  • Jen Foyle, President and Executive Creative Director, stated, "As the quarter progressed, we saw improvement and met our sales guidance. However, promotion activity was up, resulting in lower AURs." She identified fashion misses in Aerie and higher costs, but noted areas of strength in OFFLINE by Aerie and growing market share in intimates.

  • Mike Mathias, CFO, reported, "first quarter consolidated revenue of $1.1 billion declined 5% to last year." He detailed the $75 million inventory write-down, a gross margin of 29.6%, and an adjusted operating loss of $68 million, with adjusted loss per share of ($0.29). Mathias stated, "We ended the quarter with approximately $88 million in cash investments and over $620 million of total liquidity, including our revolver."

Outlook

  • The company has paused full-year guidance, citing the need for greater visibility, but expects second quarter revenue to "trend similar to the first quarter, with revenue down 5% and comparable sales down approximately 3%." Operating income is expected to be between $40 million and $45 million for Q2, and gross margin is projected to be down versus last year, primarily due to higher in-season markdowns and cost deleverage.

  • Full-year capital expenditures are now expected to be approximately $275 million, as the company recadences some investments.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for the first quarter was reported at $1.1 billion, a 5% decline from the previous year. Comparable sales decreased 3%. Gross profit dollars were $322 million, including the $75 million inventory write-down. Gross margin for the quarter was 29.6%.

  • SG&A dollars increased 2% due to higher advertising, while depreciation was $52 million. The company ended the quarter with approximately $88 million in cash and investments and over $620 million in total liquidity.

  • Inventory was down 5% year-over-year following the write-down.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about the macro environment and its impact on performance. Schottenstein responded, "we're optimistic. We're hoping that this tax plan gets passed sooner than later. And I think that will give a lot of optimism, and it may even stimulate the economy as far as the second-half goes."

  • Boss asked Mike Mathias about Q2 operating income and margin assumptions. Mathias replied, inventory is "in a better place for the back half, very much in line with sales expectations," and confirmed ongoing promotional activity to clear inventory for back-to-school.

  • Jay Sole, UBS: Questioned merchandising issues and speed to recovery. Foyle said, "these teams are working very swiftly to repair what our learnings are," and expressed excitement for upcoming back-to-school campaigns, citing new team members and talent.

  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about capital allocation and merchandise assortment improvements. Foyle highlighted strong denim performance and growing market share in intimates, while Mathias confirmed the CapEx reduction to $275 million, with 60 to 70 AE remodels and about 30 Aerie store openings now planned.

  • Marni Shapiro, The Retail Tracker: Sought clarity on denim strength and marketing. Foyle reported strong women's denim results and noted, "our customer file in AE has grown high-single-digits, and Aerie...has grown mid-single-digits."

  • Rick Patel, Raymond James: Inquired about promotional outlook and SG&A. Mathias expects "a little pressure on markdown through promotions" in Q2, and SG&A is expected to be "relatively flat in the year," with higher advertising spend offset by reductions elsewhere.

  • Johnna Kim, TD Cowen: Asked about digital performance and tariffs. Schottenstein noted uptick in digital, and Foyle said costs are now "in line, including the tariffs," with mitigations in place.

  • Alex Straton, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarity on reinstating guidance and key metrics. Mathias said AUR and conversion are key metrics under close watch, with focus on stabilization before reinstating annual guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts took a slightly negative to cautious tone, pressing on macro headwinds, inventory, and pace of merchandising correction. Multiple questions focused on the ability to recover from Q1 missteps, speed of product improvement, and effectiveness of promotional strategies.

  • Management maintained a tone of urgency and guarded optimism. Schottenstein stated, "we are working with urgency to reignite our performance," while Mathias and Foyle emphasized ongoing corrective actions and confidence in back-to-school preparations.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment shifted from confidence and growth focus to defensive explanations and a more urgent, corrective tone addressing recent setbacks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a previously provided full-year outlook to a pause, with management now offering only Q2 expectations.

  • Q1 2025 revealed a notable decline in revenue and operating results versus Q4 2024, which had highlighted record performance. The current quarter also included a $75 million inventory write-down and a $68 million operating loss, a reversal from the prior quarter's $142 million operating income.

  • Strategic priorities have shifted to urgent corrective actions, inventory management, and more cautious capital allocation, reflected in the reduced CapEx outlook. The previous quarter's tone of growth and market share gains has given way to focus on operational discipline and margin recovery.

  • Analysts' questions this quarter focused more on short-term risk management and execution, contrasting with last quarter's growth and brand expansion themes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited product misses, higher costs, and promotional pressure as key drivers of margin decline.

  • $75 million inventory write-down on spring and summer goods was a material setback.

  • Uncertainties around tariffs continue, but management outlined mitigation efforts and supply chain adjustments, including diversifying away from China.

  • The pause in annual guidance underscores ongoing uncertainty, with management citing macro headwinds and the need for greater visibility.

Final Takeaway

American Eagle management acknowledged a challenging first quarter marked by margin pressure, inventory correction, and paused annual guidance. The leadership team emphasized swift corrective actions, a more disciplined inventory approach, and an urgent focus on operational improvements to position the company for a stronger back-to-school season and beyond. While near-term challenges remain, the company is leveraging its brand strength, recalibrating capital allocation, and targeting a return to profitable growth as macro headwinds stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NetApp outlines $6.75B revenue midpoint for FY26 as AI and all-flash drive growth amid macro caution
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NetApp, Inc. (NTAP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO George Kurian reported record revenue for the fourth quarter and fiscal year 2025, attributing the results to strong growth and significant market share gains in all-flash storage, along with accelerating growth in first party and marketplace storage services. Kurian stated, "We achieved all-time highs for gross profit, operating profit, operating margin, and EPS in fiscal year 2025, a clear indication of our ability to navigate a dynamic environment."
  • The company highlighted that all-flash array annualized revenue run rate reached $4.1 billion, growing 14% from Q4 a year ago, with all-flash now representing over two-thirds of hybrid cloud segment revenue. NetApp gained almost 300 basis points of all-flash market share in calendar 2024 as reported by IDC.
  • The AI business grew five-fold year-over-year in the fourth quarter, with approximately 150 AI infrastructure and data lake modernization deals closed. Kurian indicated, "In the fourth quarter, our AI business grew five-fold year-over-year, again performing ahead of plan."
  • Kurian introduced Wissam Jabre as the new CFO, noting his deep knowledge of the market and track record of value creation.
  • CFO Wissam Jabre stated, "Total revenue for Q4 came in slightly above the midpoint of our guidance range at $1.73 billion, up 4% year-over-year and up 6% sequentially. Q4 billings of $2.03 billion were up 12% year-over-year. This marks our sixth consecutive quarter of year-over-year revenue and billings growth."

Outlook

  • NetApp expects fiscal year 2026 total revenue in the range of $6.625 billion to $6.875 billion, with a midpoint of $6.75 billion, reflecting 3% year-over-year growth. Excluding the divested Spot business, total revenue guidance implies 4% growth year-over-year.
  • Consolidated gross margin is projected at 71% to 72%, and operating margin is expected to be in the range of 28.8% to 29.8%. EPS is expected in the range of $7.60 to $7.90, with a midpoint of $7.75.
  • For Q1 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected between $1.455 billion and $1.605 billion, with a midpoint implying a decline of 1% year-over-year, or 1% growth when excluding Spot. Q1 gross margin is projected at 71% to 72%, with operating margin at 25% to 26%. EPS guidance is $1.48 to $1.58. The company intends to return up to 100% of free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.
  • Kurian stated, "We expect some increased spending caution, as well as on-going friction in U.S. Public Sector and EMEA. We are incorporating an appropriate layer of caution in our outlook due to these factors."

Financial Results

  • Q4 product revenue was $845 million, up 5% year-over-year. Support revenue was $625 million, flat year-over-year, and professional services revenue was $98 million, up 13% year-over-year, driven by Keystone.
  • Public Cloud revenue reached $164 million, up 8% year-over-year; excluding the divested Spot business, Public Cloud revenue grew 22% year-over-year.
  • Q4 consolidated gross margin was 69.5%. Total Hybrid Cloud gross margin was 68.4%, with product gross margin at 55.4%. Public Cloud gross margin was 79.3%, up 290 basis points sequentially and 11 percentage points year-over-year.
  • Operating expenses were $707 million, down 2% year-over-year. Q4 operating margin was 28.6%, and EPS was $1.93, up 7% year-over-year. Cash flow from operations was $675 million, and free cash flow was $640 million.
  • NetApp announced an increase in its share repurchase authorization by $1.1 billion.

Q&A

  • Tim Long, Barclays, asked about visibility into accelerating revenues and EPS growth through the year and product gross margins outlook. Kurian responded that growth is expected from large AI infrastructure deals and added sales capacity, while Jabre indicated product gross margins are expected to improve gradually during the year.
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan, questioned if deal timing issues persisted and sought clarity on public cloud growth ex-Spot. Kurian noted most deals that slipped from Q3 closed in Q4 and that public cloud revenue ex-Spot was up 22% year-over-year in Q4.
  • Wamsi Mohan, Bank of America, inquired about large AI deals and tariff impacts. Kurian stated, "Within our FY ’26 outlook, tariffs are about 40 to 60 basis points of our gross margin, so a small amount."
  • Analysts repeatedly asked about macro caution, AI momentum, margin drivers, product portfolio traction, and the impact of tariffs and public sector exposure. Management provided specific details, with Jabre stating, "We anticipate improvement from there, gradual improvement into the rest of the fiscal year on a quarterly basis."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally neutral to slightly cautious, focusing on macro uncertainties, deal pacing, and margin sustainability, but also probing for AI and product momentum.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing record results, execution, and market share gains. During Q&A, the tone shifted to more defensive and prudent language, particularly regarding macro risks and public sector exposure. Kurian stated, "We feel that it's important to be prudent in our outlook, just given the range of volatility that's going on."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence appeared more measured, with greater emphasis on macro caution, while analysts remained focused on growth drivers and potential headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 marked continued revenue and billings growth, with all-flash and AI segments accelerating, compared to Q3’s lower top-line growth and sales execution concerns.
  • Guidance language shifted to incorporate more macro caution, particularly regarding U.S. Public Sector and EMEA.
  • Operating margin remained strong, but Q4’s 28.6% was slightly below Q3’s 30%. EPS growth continued, but management signaled a cautious start to fiscal 2026, unlike the previous quarter’s more optimistic tone.
  • Analysts’ focus changed from execution issues and deal slippage in Q3 to macroeconomic uncertainty and sustainability of AI-led momentum in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited mixed macroeconomic signals, lingering inflation, and increased global uncertainty as factors influencing their cautious outlook.
  • Specific challenges included public sector budget delays, EMEA instability, and tariffs, with Kurian noting exposure to tariffs was limited due to the company’s diversified supply chain.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of AI momentum, margin improvement, cloud revenue growth ex-Spot, and the impact of geopolitical and trade policies.

Final Takeaway

NetApp closed fiscal year 2025 with record highs in revenue, profit metrics, and AI business growth, emphasizing its leadership in all-flash and public cloud storage. While management projects continued growth for fiscal 2026, they have incorporated added caution in their guidance due to macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties, particularly in public sector and EMEA markets. The company remains focused on capitalizing on enterprise AI and expanding its all-flash and cloud offerings, expecting these segments to drive sustained performance in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon expects victory in dispute over Chevron-Hess deal, exec says
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) is confident it will prevail in its contractual dispute over Chevron's (CVX) planned acquisition of Hess (HES), Exxon Senior VP Neil Chapman said Thursday at a Bernstein conference in New York.

Arbitration proceedings concluded this week and a decision is expected in 2-3 months, and Chapman said he is confident the three-member panel will rule in Exxon's (NYSE:XOM) favor and determine it has a right-of-first-refusal to purchase Hess' (HES) stake in the prolific Guyana oil joint venture operated by Exxon.

But there will be no change if Chevron (CVX) wins in arbitration and gains entry into the Stabroek block, Chapman said, even while expressing confidence that Exxon (XOM) will prevail.

"If the judges decide that's not the case, then we get a new partner," Chapman said. "Business carries on as normal."

Chevron (CVX) agreed to buy Hess (HES) in a $53B deal in October 2023 - with Hess' 30% stake in the Stabroek block off the coast of Guyana the prize asset - but the deal was delayed when Exxon (XOM) filed arbitration claims last year asserting pre-emptive rights to Hess' interest in the project.

data.title: UiPath outlines $1.549B–$1.554B FY26 revenue target as Agentic Automation gains traction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UiPath Inc. (PATH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Dines highlighted the momentum following the launch of the Agentic Automation platform, describing it as "one of the most important and successful product introductions in our history." Dines noted, "The momentum following our launch has been exciting and was matched by strong first quarter financial performance." He attributed the results to the expansion of Agentic automation and orchestration capabilities and emphasized engagement with customers around these solutions.
  • Dines reported quarterly revenue of $357 million and annualized recurring revenue (ARR) of $1.693 billion, stating, "We executed with discipline and focus in what continues to be a variable macroeconomical environment, exceeding expectations on the top and bottom-line."
  • Dines detailed several high-impact events, including the Agentic AI Summit and DevCon 2025, which attracted over 15,000 participants. He cited rapid adoption for new offerings: "Since launching Agent Builder in preview this January, we've seen accelerating adoption with customers creating thousands of autonomous agents and generating over 250,000 agent runs today."
  • Dines also referenced a "multiyear, multimillion dollar expansion" with a Fortune 15 health company and significant partnerships, including a new AI partnership with Google Cloud and expanded collaboration with Microsoft.
  • CFO Ashim Gupta stated, "We had a solid start to the year with first quarter revenue growing $357 million, an increase of 6%. ARR totaled $1.693 billion, an increase of 12%, driven by first quarter net new ARR of $27 million."
  • Gupta described the launch of a new partner program and the completion of the company’s transformation initiative, noting, "We have substantially completed the overall transformation which has yielded significant operating leverage as seen in our results."

Outlook

  • Gupta provided Q2 2026 guidance of revenue in the range of $345 million to $350 million and ARR in the range of $1.715 billion to $1.720 billion. Full fiscal year 2026 guidance is revenue of $1.549 billion to $1.554 billion, ARR of $1.820 billion to $1.825 billion, non-GAAP operating income of approximately $305 million, and non-GAAP adjusted free cash flow of approximately $370 million.
  • Gupta stated, "We are encouraged by early traction with our newly launched Agentic Solutions. However, adoption is still in its early phases, and as such, we don't expect a material revenue contribution in fiscal 2026."
  • Guidance was described as prudent given ongoing macroeconomic variability, with an updated view on revenue linearity due to deal timing and mix between Q2 and Q3.

Financial Results

  • First quarter revenue was $357 million; ARR reached $1.693 billion, with net new ARR of $27 million.
  • Non-GAAP operating income for the quarter was $70 million, representing a 20% margin, and non-GAAP adjusted free cash flow was $117 million.
  • Gross margin was 84%, with software gross margin at 90%. Operating expenses were $231 million. GAAP operating loss improved to $16 million.
  • The company ended the quarter with $1.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, and no debt. 21.9 million shares were repurchased at an average price of $10.40.
  • Dollar-based gross retention was 97%, with a dollar-based net retention rate of 108%. Remaining performance obligations increased to $1.231 billion.

Q&A

  • Sheldon McMeans, Barclays: Asked about macroeconomic uncertainty and FX impact. Gupta responded, "The macroeconomic environment, it is still variable... we kind of adjusted our linearity as such, which for me is a favorable move from a revenue standpoint." On FX: "It really was pretty minimal... nothing significant or material that we would factor in."
  • McMeans, Barclays: Questioned feedback on unified pricing and platform units. Dines said, "The initial reaction is positive because the new pricing model is tied closer to the adoption of the software."
  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: Inquired about U.S. federal performance. Dines noted, "There is a really enthusiasm about it what Agentic Automation can bring... but at the same time, we see... continued pressure on the new budgets being finalized."
  • Murphy, JPMorgan: Asked about pipeline quality. Gupta responded, "We feel good about the quality... what bolsters that confidence is just the activity that we have on the Agentic front."
  • Vik Robers, William Blair: Asked about Maestro orchestration differentiation. Dines described Maestro as offering "a very unique proposition in the market" by connecting agents with robots and people for enterprise-level workflows.
  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen: Sought clarification on deal timing and margin leverage. Gupta said, "The shift in the deal mix is favorable... we're encouraged by the activity of what's moving into second quarter."
  • Terry Tillman, Truist: Probed on competitive displacement. Dines indicated, "There are traditional RPA vendors that cannot keep the pace with the innovation."
  • Siti Panigrahi, Mizuho: Questioned net retention rate trajectory. Gupta explained, "We see POCs particularly on the Agentic side and no material impact to revenue."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing questions on macro uncertainty, deal timing, and product adoption, while seeking clarity on customer retention and pipeline quality.
  • Management's tone was confident in prepared remarks, emphasizing innovation and operational progress, but more cautious in the Q&A, reflecting ongoing macroeconomic and budgetary headwinds. Dines expressed, "I've never been more confident about the path we are on," but Gupta repeatedly referenced "prudent" guidance and variable conditions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence regarding Agentic Automation adoption was more pronounced, while analysts remained similarly focused on macro impacts and growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 revenue was $357 million, compared to $424 million in Q4 2025. ARR grew from $1.666 billion to $1.693 billion. Non-GAAP operating income was $70 million in Q1 2026, compared to $134 million in Q4 2025.
  • Net retention rate declined from 110% in Q4 2025 to 108% in Q1 2026, and gross retention moved from 98% to 97%.
  • Management in both quarters referenced macroeconomic headwinds and public sector transitions, but the current quarter included more concrete examples of Agentic Automation driving new deals and customer expansion.
  • Analysts’ questions continued to center on public sector performance, deal linearity, and adoption of new solutions, but there was increased emphasis on the monetization and competitive differentiation of the Agentic platform.
  • Guidance for full-year revenue was raised to $1.549 billion–$1.554 billion in Q1 2026 from $1.525 billion–$1.530 billion previously.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic variability and public sector budget finalizations as primary risks.
  • Prudent guidance reflects continued uncertainty, with Gupta stating, "We continue to have a prudent economic environment."
  • Customer retention pressure was attributed to macro factors and federal transitions, with hopes for stabilization as conditions improve.

Final Takeaway

UiPath management emphasized the strong launch and early customer adoption of the Agentic Automation platform as a foundation for future growth, while setting expectations for modest near-term revenue contribution from these solutions. The company is guiding to higher full-year revenue and continues to focus on operational efficiency and strategic partnerships, even as macroeconomic and public sector variables persist. Leadership remains confident in the company’s positioning and innovation, viewing fiscal 2026 as a crucial year for laying groundwork for broader Agentic Automation adoption in fiscal 2027 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil ends lower as expected OPEC output hike trumps gains from tariff ruling
data.description:
Crude oil futures fell Thursday after surrendering early gains that followed the U.S. Court of International Trade's ruling that President Trump overstepped his authority by imposing across-the-board tariffs on imports from U.S. trading partners.

Oil futures steadily retreated throughout the session, however, as Trump administration officials downplayed the impact of the ruling and hinted at other legal avenues to employ trade policy.

After regular trading ended, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which oversees the International Trade Court, granted Trump's request for a temporary administrative stay of the ruling.

One explanation of oil's reversal, Jim Ritterbusch of Ritterbusch and Associates said in a note, "could be that not much has changed and that the uncertainty surrounding the Trump tariffs from day one will continue as the tariffs work their way through the court system and several sectoral tariffs such as autos and auto parts remain intact."

Oil has been drifting lower on expectations that OPEC+ will agree to add another 411K bbl/day to July production targets when the group meets this weekend.

Analysts say the OPEC output hike likely will continue adding downward pressure to crude oil prices over the medium term, although oil demand has held up relatively well compared to expectations and may help absorb some of the increased production.

Oil futures trimmed some losses after the Energy Information Administration data reported U.S. crude inventories posted a surprise draw in the latest week, falling by 2.8M barrels, largely on higher exports, while gasoline stocks fell by 2.4M barrels and distillate stocks dropped by 700K barrels.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for July delivery closed -1.4% to $60.94/bbl, front-month July Brent crude (CO1:COM) ended -1.1% to $64.15/bbl, the largest one-day dollar and percentage declines in two weeks, while July front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) settled -1% at $3.522/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Marvell forecasts $2B Q2 revenue amid accelerating AI-driven data center momentum and custom silicon growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Matt Murphy highlighted record revenue of $1.895 billion for Q1 2026, noting a 4% sequential increase and 63% year-over-year growth, stating, "Data center end market continued to deliver strong growth, driven by robust AI demand." Murphy emphasized ongoing recovery in carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking, and announced a significant stock repurchase of $340 million in Q1, up from $200 million in the prior quarter.
  • Murphy detailed the sale of the Automotive Ethernet business to Infineon for $2.5 billion, describing it as an all-cash transaction that "will provide us with additional flexibility in our capital allocation strategy." He also noted the launch of new custom silicon and advanced packaging innovations, including a partnership with NVIDIA for NVLink Fusion technology.
  • Murphy reported that Marvell's lead XPU program for a large US hyperscale data center customer "has become a key revenue driver for our custom business" and confirmed securing 3-nanometer wafer and packaging capacity, with production expected to start in calendar 2026. Murphy stated, "We anticipate that our revenue from custom AI XPUs for this customer will continue to grow next year, fiscal 2027 and beyond."
  • CFO Willem Meintjes said, "Revenue in the first quarter was $1.895 billion exceeding the midpoint of our guidance, growing 63% year-over-year and 4% sequentially. Data center was our largest end market contributing 76% of total revenue." Meintjes added, "Non-GAAP earnings per diluted share was $0.62 reflecting year-over-year growth of 158%, which is more than double the pace of revenue growth."

Outlook

  • Marvell is forecasting Q2 2026 revenue of $2 billion at the midpoint of guidance, with Murphy stating, "This represents 57% year-over-year growth and would set another record revenue level for Marvell."
  • Meintjes provided detailed guidance: "We expect our GAAP gross margin to be between 50% and 51%. We expect our non-GAAP gross margin to be between 59% and 60%." Additionally, "We anticipate GAAP earnings per diluted share in the range of $0.16 to $0.26. We expect non-GAAP earnings per diluted share in the range of $0.62 to $0.72."
  • Management expects continued sequential growth in data center revenue and mid-single-digit percentage sequential growth in enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure.

Financial Results

  • Record Q1 revenue of $1.895 billion, a 4% increase sequentially and 63% year-over-year.
  • Data center revenue reached $1.44 billion, growing 5% sequentially and 76% year-over-year, comprising 76% of total revenue.
  • GAAP gross margin was 50.3% and non-GAAP gross margin was 59.8%. Non-GAAP operating margin was 34.2%.
  • GAAP earnings per diluted share was $0.20; non-GAAP earnings per diluted share was $0.62.
  • Cash flow from operations was $333 million. Inventory stood at $1.07 billion, up $42 million from the prior quarter. Shareholder returns included $52 million in dividends and $340 million in stock repurchases.
  • Total debt was $4.2 billion, with a gross debt to EBITDA ratio of 1.8 times.

Q&A

  • Vivek Arya, Bank of America, asked about content direction and exclusivity for next-generation 3-nanometer XPUs. Murphy stated, "We're the incumbent shipping the current generation of this AI XPU...we have secured 3-nanometer wafer and advanced packaging capacity, and that's for 2026 where we expect to start production." Murphy noted, "It's certainly possible and likely that customers...may be pursuing multiple paths to meet their requirements."
  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank, questioned Marvell's ability to support a broader customer base. Murphy responded, "We absolutely have the capacity engineering-wise to expand our portfolio and expand our engagements."
  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel, asked about Marvell's SerDes technology and the NVIDIA partnership. Murphy explained, "Our SerDes technology remains best-in-class, both from a performance standpoint, as well as a time-to-market standpoint." On NVIDIA, Murphy said, "It's a sign that it demonstrates how...we're deeply engaged in the ecosystem, Tore, with key partners."
  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS, inquired about the breakdown of data center revenue and AI growth. Murphy replied, "AI had crossed and was now the majority of our data center revenue...we see a path here in the not-too-distant future where AI...becomes the majority of HoldCo."
  • Several analysts questioned the sustainability of custom silicon growth, gross margins, and optical business momentum. Murphy and Meintjes reiterated expectations for continued growth and outlined the impact of custom business mix on margin dynamics.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts voiced recurring concerns about customer concentration, exclusivity, and the sustainability of gross margins, with a slightly skeptical tone as seen in questions about competitive dynamics and content direction.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and the Q&A, frequently reaffirming growth drivers and providing clarity on supply chain and competitive positioning. Murphy used phrases such as "We are extremely well positioned" and "We feel very confident and very good about our revenue continuity."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism about customer concentration and competitive risks persisted, while management’s tone remained confident, focusing on execution and visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 revenue rose from $1.817 billion in Q4 2025 to $1.895 billion in Q1 2026, with data center revenue increasing from $1.37 billion to $1.44 billion.
  • Non-GAAP EPS increased from $0.60 to $0.62, and non-GAAP operating margin expanded from 33.7% to 34.2%.
  • Stock repurchases accelerated from $200 million to $340 million.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize a $2 billion Q2 revenue target, compared to the previous quarter’s forecast of $1.875 billion for Q1.
  • Management continued to signal confidence in custom silicon growth and highlighted new partnerships and product innovations.
  • Analysts remained focused on the risk of dual sourcing, gross margin impacts from custom business, and the sustainability of AI-driven revenue growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Murphy acknowledged ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties and the potential for customers to pursue multiple sourcing strategies.
  • Management addressed questions about gross margin volatility due to the custom business mix and highlighted monitoring of broader market conditions.
  • Analysts repeatedly raised concerns about competitive dynamics, customer exclusivity, and the pace of AI-related growth.

Final Takeaway

Marvell Technology began fiscal 2026 with record quarterly revenue and expanding profitability, driven by robust demand for AI in data centers and continued recovery across enterprise and carrier markets. Management highlighted significant milestones in custom silicon programs, ongoing innovation in advanced packaging, and a major divestiture to enhance capital flexibility. The company forecasts another record quarter ahead, with a clear focus on scaling its custom AI silicon and interconnect solutions, while navigating competitive and macroeconomic challenges in pursuit of sustained long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
